metadata.title: How Music Mutates: A Logical History of Genre

metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Seth Brown — Humanitarians AI Fellow. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Genres don't invent themselves; each mutates from an earlier form by borrowing and altering its underlying logic. The blues is the root, seeding almost every genre that followed through its structural and rhythmic DNA. Electronic Dance Music inherited disco's rhythmic logic while stripping away its production budget and human feel. The drum machine ran that same disco logic mechanically, showing mutation as constraint carried forward, not creation from nothing.

artifactLines: Genres don't invent themselves; each mutates from an earlier form by borrowing and altering its underlying logic.
The blues is the root, seeding almost every genre that followed through its structural and rhythmic DNA.
Electronic Dance Music inherited disco's rhythmic logic while stripping away its production budget and human feel.
The drum machine ran that same disco logic mechanically, showing mutation as constraint carried forward, not creation from nothing.

beats.narration_text: The framing that genres don't invent themselves, and that the blues seeded almost everything, reframed music history for me as inheritance rather than creation. The EDM example, where the drum machine ran disco's rhythmic logic without its budget or human feel, is what I want to trace. Can you (1) diagram the logical descent from blues to EDM as a lineage of borrowed constraints, (2) pinpoint exactly which disco element the drum machine preserved and which it discarded, and (3) predict what the next mutation from EDM would logically inherit?

Start with the blues, because the blues started almost everything
